<Summary source="https://arabcenterdc.org/resource/sudans-war-the-failure-of-mediation-and-the-struggle-for-civilian-rule/">
## Summary of Article on the Sudanese Civil War

**Note:** The article appears to be cut off at the end, so the summary reflects only the available content.

### Key Facts & Timeline

- **War began:** April 15, 2023, between the SAF (led by al-Burhan) and RSF (led by "Hemedti"/Dagalo)
- **Dual governments by 2025:** SAF's "Hope Government" in Port Sudan (east); RSF's "Government of Peace and Unity" in Nyala, Darfur (west)
- **March 2025:** SAF recaptured Khartoum, including presidential palace, airport, and army headquarters — described as a major symbolic and strategic turning point
- **October 26, 2025:** RSF captured al-Fasher, the last major Darfur city outside its control, giving it control of all of Darfur
- **De facto partition:** RSF controls west/southwest Sudan; SAF controls east and Khartoum

### Humanitarian Crisis

- **11.8 million forcibly displaced** (7.4M internally displaced; 4.2M fled to neighboring countries) — the world's largest displacement crisis
- Sudan ranks among the **top four nations globally for acute malnutrition**, with over half the population suffering severe hunger
- UN Human Rights Council Fact-Finding Mission (September 5, 2025) found **both parties committed war crimes**, including mass killings, forced displacement, looting, destruction of hospitals and infrastructure, and systematic sexual violence
- Both sides have **weaponized food access**, targeting fertile regions and obstructing humanitarian aid

### Root Causes & Political Context

- War stems from the **2018–2019 popular uprising** that toppled al-Bashir's 30-year regime; the revolutionary movement demanded full civilian rule
- The RSF grew out of the **Janjaweed militias** responsible for genocidal violence in Darfur beginning in 2003
- Key driver: dispute over **integrating RSF into national armed forces** and control of Sudan's **gold sector**
- The **UAE** is identified as a primary destination for RSF gold smuggling and a weapons supplier to the RSF (via Chad and Libya)
- SAF has allied with **Islamists** (some tied to the former Bashir regime), complicating its democratic legitimacy claims

### Peace Prospects

- Only one ceasefire noted: a **brief one-day ceasefire on May 22, 2023**, which quickly collapsed
- The article characterizes the conflict as a war between **counterrevolutionary factions** backed by foreign actors resistant to civilian democratic governance
- The article argues any sustainable peace must address Sudanese civilians' demands for **genuine civilian, democratic governance** — demands the warring parties and foreign backers have so far rejected
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.wilsoncenter.org/blog-post/sudans-ceasefire-talks-what-has-been-missing-thus-far">
## Summary: "Sudan's Ceasefire Talks: What Has Been Missing Thus Far?" (Wilson Center, October 31, 2024)

### Key Facts & Statistics
- The Sudanese civil war has killed tens of thousands and displaced over **10 million people**, with over **2 million facing starvation**
- Fighting began **April 15, 2023** between the SAF (led by General al-Burhan) and RSF (led by "Hemedti" Dagalo)
- **May 2023 Jeddah talks** (organized by US and Saudi Arabia) stalled after both sides failed to implement the "Jeddah Declaration"
- **August 2024 Geneva talks** (US, Saudi Arabia, Switzerland, with Egypt, UAE, AU, and UN as observers): RSF attended but **SAF refused**, demanding RSF move to cantonment sites first; resulted only in humanitarian aid commitments, **no ceasefire progress**
- **Early 2024 Manama talks** reportedly made notable progress but were derailed by internal disagreements within SAF's Port Sudan government and Islamist allies

### Key Analytical Points (Wilson Center, a named/reliable source)
- Both SAF and RSF believe they can achieve **military victory**, reducing incentives for a ceasefire
- Both sides have **sustained external support**, further entrenching their positions
- A ceasefire is unlikely without first defining a **political endgame** addressing accountability, security sector reform, and governance roles
- Regional dynamics are increasingly complicated by involvement of **Iran, Turkey, Qatar, Russia, and Ukraine** alongside existing players
- Deep **civilian divisions** complicate inclusion in talks; most Sudanese civilians oppose military participation in future political institutions, yet excluding military actors won't end fighting

### Structural Obstacles Identified
1. No agreed political endgame for either belligerent or their external backers
2. Islamist/former regime supporters actively **advocate continuation of war**
3. Lack of a **cohesive civilian negotiating bloc**
4. Growing number of **competing regional actors** with irreconcilable interests

### Proposed Solutions
- Convene a **parallel civilian negotiating forum** alongside military talks
- Use civilian "Resource Persons" or "Friends of the Mediators" to bridge gaps

### Overall Assessment (Wilson Center)
> *"Unless the above shortcomings are adequately addressed, the prospects for peace emerging from renewed negotiations will remain slim."*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.dabangasudan.org/en/all-news/article/sudan-peace-talks-timeline-marked-by-repeated-failures-and-deadlock">
## Summary: Sudan Peace Talks Timeline (Dabanga Radio TV Online, July 29, 2025)

### Context
The article was published ahead of a **Quartet summit in Washington DC** involving Egypt, the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and the United States — a joint Biden-Trump-backed initiative to revive stalled peace negotiations between the SAF and RSF. The article frames this against a backdrop of **repeated negotiation failures since April 2023**, with no ceasefire having held to date.

---

### Key Facts: Timeline of Failed Negotiations

| Period | Effort | Outcome |
|--------|--------|---------|
| April–May 2023 | Eid El Fitr truce | Collapsed almost immediately |
| May 2023 | First Jeddah talks (US/Saudi-brokered) | 7-day ceasefire + 5-day extension, then collapse; SAF suspended participation May 31 |
| Oct–Dec 2023 | Jeddah/Juba talks (US, Saudi, IGAD, AU) | Only humanitarian corridor agreed; suspended December 3 |
| February 2024 | Manama meeting (SAF/RSF deputies + envoys) | No tangible outcome |
| July 2024 | Geneva UN-led talks (Envoy Lamamra) | SAF refused participation |
| 2024–2025 | UK secret talks with RSF | Shelved after political backlash from Port Sudan government |

---

### Structural Reasons for Failure (Named Analysts/Mediators)
- **Deep mutual mistrust** between SAF and RSF
- **Disjointed mediation** by multiple competing actors (US, Saudi Arabia, IGAD, AU) working at cross-purposes
- **Both sides prioritize military victory** over negotiation
- **No enforcement mechanisms** for ceasefire violations
- **Unresolved core dispute**: future integration of RSF fighters into the regular army
- **Foreign interference** undermining neutral settlement

---

### Conditions Identified as Necessary for Progress (Expert Consensus)
- Genuine political will from both parties
- A **unified mediation platform**
- Strong international oversight
- Reduced foreign interference
- Inclusion of **Sudanese civil society and civilian political actors**

---

### Notable Quotes
- RSF's Hemedti (April 2023): *"Do not negotiate with a criminal"* (referring to SAF's El Burhan)
- SAF's Lt Gen Yasir El Atta (July 2024): *"Dialogue will begin only after [RSF] withdrawal from the cities,"* even if war lasted *"a hundred years"*

---

**Overall framing**: The article presents the Sudan peace process as characterized by **structural deadlock and repeated bad faith**, with the upcoming Quartet summit representing yet another attempt to break the impasse.
</Summary>

**UN Warns of Escalating Violence in South Sudan as MSF Loses Contact with 26 Staff**
On March 2, 2026, the non-governmental organization Médecins sans frontières (MSF) reported losing contact with 26 of its employees in the Jonglei State of South Sudan, where fighting between rival factions has intensified significantly over recent weeks. The United Nations (UN) warned the week prior of a risk of returning to a 'generalized civil war'. According to the UN Mission in South Sudan (MINUSS), an attack that occurred on Sunday, March 1, 2026, in the Ruweng province, north of the country, 'resulted in dozens of deaths among civilians and local officials'. This specific attack has not been claimed, and it remains unclear whether it involved looting, ethnic reprisals, or clashes between the regular army and opposition-linked militias. The violence involves the army loyal to President Salva Kiir and militias supporting his rival, Riek Machar. The UN had previously condemned the massacre of at least 16 civilians in Jonglei State, accusing uncontrolled army elements of responsibility and warning against a general resumption of war. The conflict follows a civil war between 2013 and 2018 which ended with a power-sharing agreement that failed to resolve divisions between Kiir and Machar. The arrest and indictment of Machar one year ago have reignited confrontations. While elections originally scheduled for late 2024 have been postponed to the end of 2026, many analysts doubt they can take place by that date.

**UN Human Rights Council 61: Core Group Statement on South Sudan**
On 27 February 2026, the Core Group for South Sudan—comprising Albania, Norway, Ireland, and the United Kingdom—delivered a statement to the UN Human Rights Council regarding the country's deteriorating human rights and security situation throughout 2025. According to the group, evidence shows renewed large-scale conflict, unprecedented aerial bombardments across multiple states, mass displacement, and the continued targeting of civilians. The statement highlights the forced recruitment of boys and young men and the widespread use of sexual violence by all parties as particularly alarming. Additionally, the Core Group noted that the detentions and prosecutions of opposition leaders, interference in the judiciary, and attempts to undermine the Revitalised Agreement erode constitutional order and threaten further instability, making credible, peaceful, and inclusive elections in 2026 unlikely. The report cited a lack of transparency, resourcing, and genuine political will in establishing transitional justice institutions, emphasizing that the Hybrid Court is urgently needed to address entrenched impunity. The Core Group urged all parties to cease hostilities, ensure humanitarian access, and uphold international law, while calling for the full renewal of the Commission's mandate. The statement was published on March 02, 2026, at 13:27 via gov.uk.
Original language: en

**UN Human Rights Council 61: UK Statement for the Enhanced Interactive Dialogue on South Sudan**
On 27 February 2026, at the UN Human Rights Council 61 session, the United Kingdom delivered a statement regarding South Sudan during an Enhanced Interactive Dialogue. The UK condemned the 'stark and troubling' humanitarian situation, citing renewed conflict, indiscriminate aerial bombardment, forced recruitment, and systematic sexual violence as a weapon of war. Specific incidents were highlighted, including the killing of civilians in Pankur on 21 February, which the UK warned could constitute war crimes. The UK also expressed grave concern over politically motivated detentions of opposition leaders and the transitional government's attempts to amend the Peace Agreement, which risk 'fatally undermining' it. Furthermore, the UK criticized the transitional government's failure to establish the Hybrid Court, noting this leaves victims without justice. The UK pledged to continue pressing for accountability and supporting regional efforts to prevent further atrocities. On 2 March 2026, Commissioners were asked to prioritize international community actions to reduce harm to civilians.

**UN: Civilian Deaths in Sudan More Than Doubled in 2025 Amid Stalled Mediation**
On March 1, 2026, Volker Türk, the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, stated that the number of civilians killed in the war in Sudan more than doubled in 2025 compared to the previous year, excluding unidentified bodies and the missing, according to a report from AFP. The conflict, which began in April 2023 between the Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), has resulted in tens of thousands of deaths and displaced 11 million people, creating one of the world's worst humanitarian crises. Türk described the war as 'dirty, bloody, and absurd,' holding both the army and the RSF responsible for refusing truces and for atrocities including sexual violence, summary executions, and arbitrary detentions. He also blamed 'foreign sponsors who feed a high-tech conflict out of greed.' International mediation efforts led by the United States, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates are currently stalled, according to US envoy Massad Boulos. The conflict has intensified with increased use of long-range drones and bombings targeting 'schools, hospitals, and places of worship' in populated areas. Specific allegations include the United Arab Emirates providing weapons to the RSF (a claim the UAE government denies) and the Sudanese army utilizing drones supplied by Iran. Despite being one of the world's poorest countries, Sudan possesses rich subsoil resources including gold and oil, fertile lands along the Nile, and over 800 km of coastline.

**The Struggle for Peace Amidst Ongoing Conflict and Humanitarian Disaster**
As of 2026, South Sudan, the world's youngest country since gaining independence in 2011, faces a resurgent civil war and severe humanitarian crisis following the collapse of the 2018 R-ARCSS peace agreement. The conflict, originally triggered in December 2013 by President Salva Kiir's accusation against First Vice President Riek Machar of a coup, evolved into an ethnic clash between the Dinka (35% of the population) and Nuer (15% of the population). According to UN estimates from 2018, the previous phase of the war had already claimed 400,000 lives and displaced four million people. Recent escalations in early 2026 include the detention of Machar in March 2025, the dismissal of his wife, Angelina Teny, from the Ministry of Interior on January 19, 2026, and General Simon Gatwech Dual's declaration on February 2, 2026, that he was withdrawing from the Port Sudan Peace Agreement to resume hostilities. Gatwech accused Kiir of sabotaging peace, while opposition factions, including the SPLM/A-IO and autonomous Nuer militias known as the 'White Army,' have seized Pajut and positions in Akobo county. The government alleges the White Army is an armed wing of the opposition, a claim the opposition denies. The humanitarian toll has intensified, with over 1.9 million internally displaced and 2.3 million refugees as of mid-2025; currently, approximately 9.3 million people (out of a total population of 11-13 million) require aid, including 7.7 million facing food shortages and 2.3 million children at risk of acute malnutrition. The crisis has prompted evacuations of civilians and UN personnel from Jonglei State, the disappearance of Doctors Without Borders staff following an airstrike, and regional instability affecting Uganda, Ethiopia, and Sudan. While the African Union and IGAD call for dialogue, the December elections, if held, are viewed by opposition leaders as rigged and likely to provoke a return to full-scale war.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 01, 2026 12:43 PM

**UNTSO Head Highlights Evolution, Challenges, and Future Role of First UN Peacekeeping Mission**
Patrick Gusha, the head of the United Nations Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO), stated that the organization, established in May 1948 as the first-ever UN peacekeeping mission, has undergone continuous development over the decades. Headquartered in Jerusalem, UNTSO military observers monitor ceasefire agreements, oversee truces, and prevent the escalation of isolated incidents in the Middle East, with some agreements evolving into peace treaties. According to the UN Information Center, Gusha noted that 'major changes' witnessed in the Middle East over the past two and a half years required significant adaptation by the organization. Gusha emphasized that due to 'hundreds of violations occurring daily,' the mission had to enforce greater restraint on parties and demonstrate the presence of oversight to ensure compliance with war rules and investigate potential war crimes. The mission also provides reassurance to populations remaining in conflict zones. Gusha highlighted coordination with other UN peacekeeping forces, specifically the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL) and the United Nations Disengagement Observer Force (UNDOF). He clarified that while UNTSO observers can monitor, report, patrol, inspect, verify, investigate, and coordinate, they cannot perform security, encirclement, evacuation, engineering, medical, or demining tasks carried out by troops; instead, 'memorandums of understanding' between missions ensure mutual understanding of roles. Gusha noted that UNIFIL's mandate was renewed for one last time until December 31, 2026, with a planned orderly and safe drawdown beginning from that date within one year, sparking 'ongoing discussions' about UNTSO potentially assuming a larger role as UNIFIL operations decrease. Addressing funding shortages faced by peacekeeping missions over the past two years, Gusha suggested that if UNTSO maintains lightness, flexibility, and enhanced reporting capabilities, there is a future possibility of utilizing this military monitoring tool more extensively. He also cited testimonies from member states and stressed the critical importance of training for military observers, asserting that field monitoring is effective only when the system functions correctly and training is successful.

**Sudan's Divided Quad and the Marginalized Will of the People: A 2026 Analysis of Proxy War and Mediation Failures**
According to an analysis published in the Sudanese newspaper 'Altaghyeer' on February 28, 2026, the internal conflict in Sudan, which began in April 2023 between the Sudanese Armed Forces led by Abdel Fattah al-Burhan and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) led by Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo (Hemedti), has evolved into a proxy war involving regional and international axes. The article argues that the 'Quad' mediation framework (the United States, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and the UK) has fractured over the question of Sudan's future state structure. The Egyptian-Saudi axis, described as adopting 'pragmatism,' allegedly favors a transitional arrangement that retains elements of the old system, viewing al-Burhan as a partner who can gradually shed hardliners. Conversely, the UAE maintains an absolute rejection of political Islam, viewing al-Burhan as a facade for Islamist networks. The article reports on a qualitative shift in the conflict, citing international reports of Turkish drones being operated from Egyptian territory to conduct strikes inside Sudan, indicating a direct operational involvement that has turned Sudan into a cross-border battlefield. The analysis highlights that the Sudanese civilian population, which drove the December 2018/2019 revolution, is being marginalized in negotiations that prioritize 'who holds the weapons' over democratic legitimacy. The author contends that the fragmentation of Sudanese civilian forces has allowed military elites and external actors to shape the future without a strong civilian partner. The article concludes by proposing an optimal scenario requiring a comprehensive roadmap with five integrated packages: a verifiable ceasefire with monitoring and humanitarian corridors; an independent transitional government of technocrats without military or Islamist figures; genuine security reform to unify the military and dismantle parallel structures; transitional justice including asset recovery and victim compensation; and the construction of a sustainable democracy with a constitution protecting freedoms and preventing coups. The piece asserts that the conflict is a struggle between a democratic civil project and a military-Islamist project backed by regional interests, and that restoring 'the will of the people' is a technical and political necessity to end the war.

Publish date: February 28, 2026 07:33 PM

**Sudanese Evaluation of U.S. Envoy Massad Boulos’s Moves**
The United States, leading the quartet comprising Saudi Arabia, Egypt, and the UAE, is preparing to present a humanitarian ceasefire proposal to the United Nations on Wednesday, following its initial roadmap for resolving the Sudanese crisis in September 2025. That plan, which called for a three-month humanitarian truce followed by a permanent ceasefire and a nine-month transitional period, was rejected by both warring parties. According to U.S. Special Advisor for African and Arab Affairs, Massad Boulos, 'serious talks on Sudan will take place in the coming days,' and the U.S. is ready to use sanctions and other tools if necessary. In a speech at the Munich Security Conference on February 13, 2026, Boulos affirmed the U.S. commitment to press for a ceasefire, with a UN meeting scheduled for next week to shape the framework for future discussions. President Donald Trump, Boulos noted, is 'concerned and wants the war in Sudan to end as soon as possible.' Sudanese government sources confirmed that five responses have been submitted to the quartet’s proposal, but the remaining documents no longer reflect the group’s position. The government’s response is based on its own peace plan, previously submitted to the UN in March 2025 and reaffirmed by Prime Minister Khalil Ibrahim at the UN General Assembly in September 2025 and at the Munich Security Conference. At the latter, Ibrahim stated the government has no concrete ceasefire proposal or comprehensive peace plan on the table, emphasizing that a full ceasefire must be accompanied by the withdrawal and disarmament of armed groups and security arrangements to prevent a resurgence of violence. The 'Quintuple Mechanism'—comprising the African Union, IGAD, the EU, the UN, and the Arab League—has intensified efforts to achieve a political transition to civilian rule, calling for immediate action to halt escalation, prevent further casualties, and ensure humanitarian access, especially as Ramadan approaches. The mechanism held separate consultations with the Democratic Forces of the Revolution (Sudanese Civilian Alliance 'Sudan') led by Abdalla Hamdok, the Rapid Support Forces-led Sudanese Foundation Alliance (Taswiyya), and pro-army political factions. The 'Sudan' alliance presented a vision linking ceasefire, humanitarian, and political tracks, while the alliance stressed coordination between the quintuple mechanism and the quartet. Analyst Abbas Mohamed Saleh criticized the quartet’s initiative for failing to produce tangible breakthroughs, arguing that the humanitarian crisis has been weaponized and ceasefire calls used as political and media tools to impose moral and humanitarian costs on Khartoum. He contends that regional and international mechanisms have proven limited, lacking neutrality and failing to address external actors fueling the conflict through local proxies, thereby deepening the crisis. Political analyst Khalid Abdullah believes Boulos faces mounting pressure to deliver progress after stalled efforts, and any new failure could undermine his credibility amid criticism from former U.S. diplomats and experts. Despite Boulos’s optimistic statements, Abdullah notes that communications with the army and RSF leadership remain strained due to divergent demands over humanitarian conditions, the absence of a comprehensive resolution vision, and internal inconsistencies within the quartet’s positions.

**Five International Organizations Issue Joint Statement Urging Sudanese Factions to Cease Fire**
On February 11, 2026, the United Nations, African Union, Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD), Arab League, and European Union issued a joint statement urging all warring parties in Sudan to immediately cease hostilities and implement a humanitarian ceasefire during the upcoming Ramadan period to protect civilian lives. The statement expressed deep concern over the rapidly deteriorating humanitarian situation in Sudan’s Kordofan region and Blue Nile State, calling on all parties to protect civilians and civilian infrastructure, respect international humanitarian law, and ensure safe, swift, and unimpeded access for humanitarian aid to all affected areas. It emphasized that serious violations of international humanitarian law must not go unpunished and that perpetrators must be held accountable. The statement also urged all relevant parties to seize the opportunity presented by ongoing diplomatic efforts to achieve a Ramadan ceasefire, reduce the intensity of hostilities immediately, and prevent further loss of life. It reaffirmed firm support for Sudan’s sovereignty, unity, independence, and territorial integrity. The conflict between Sudan’s Armed Forces and the Rapid Support Forces began on April 15, 2023, in the capital Khartoum, spreading to other regions and resulting in nearly 30,000 deaths over more than two years of fighting. (CCTV reporter Zhao Yufang)

**Five-Nation Committee Urges Immediate Ceasefire in Sudan Amid Escalating Humanitarian Crisis**
The five-member Sudan Coordination Committee—comprising the African Union, the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD), the Arab League, the European Union, and the United Nations—issued a formal statement on February 12, 2026, expressing deep concern over the escalating conflict in Sudan. The committee called for an immediate halt to further military escalation, including the use of highly destructive weapons, which have caused severe harm to civilians. It highlighted a rapid deterioration in humanitarian conditions in Kordofan and Blue Nile states, citing reports of drone strikes resulting in casualties, intensified sieges on populated areas, attacks on vital civilian infrastructure such as hospitals, schools, and humanitarian assets, forced displacement, and severe restrictions on aid access, including threats to major supply routes and attacks on relief convoys. The committee referenced the tragic events in El Fasher, noting prior warnings that went unheeded, leading to devastating consequences for civilians. It stressed that protecting civilians, civilian facilities, and critical national infrastructure is a fundamental obligation under international law, and affirmed that international humanitarian law applies to all parties involved in the conflict. The committee emphasized the need for accountability for serious violations, the importance of ensuring safe, rapid, and unimpeded humanitarian access to all affected areas, and the necessity of coordinated, effective collective efforts—particularly from influential actors—to de-escalate tensions, halt the flow of weapons and fighters, and prevent further state fragmentation. With the approach of Ramadan, the committee urged all parties to seize the opportunity to establish a humanitarian truce, immediately cease hostilities, and facilitate life-saving aid delivery. It affirmed that such measures must align with international law, international humanitarian law, existing commitments, and relevant UN Security Council resolutions, particularly Resolution 2736 adopted on June 13, 2024, which it described as a potential stepping stone toward a broader cessation of hostilities. The committee reaffirmed its commitment to Sudan’s sovereignty, unity, independence, and territorial integrity, warning that continued escalation undermines civilian protection and national infrastructure. It concluded by reiterating its commitment to facilitating a comprehensive, nationally owned, and inclusive Sudanese-Sudanese political dialogue aimed at ending the war and laying the foundation for a peaceful political transition.

**UN, EU, AU, IGAD, and Arab League Demand Humanitarian Truce in Sudan Amid Escalating Conflict**
The United Nations (UN), European Union (EU), African Union (AU), Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD), and League of Arab States jointly issued a statement expressing deep concern over the escalating conflict in Sudan. They called for an immediate cessation of military escalation, including the use of increasingly destructive weapons, which are causing devastating harm to civilians. The organizations highlighted particular alarm over the rapid deterioration in Kordofan and Blue Nile states, where reports detail deadly drone attacks, tightening sieges on populated areas, and attacks on critical civilian infrastructure such as hospitals, schools, and humanitarian supply routes. Forced displacements and severe restrictions on humanitarian access—such as threats to key supply corridors and attacks on aid convoys—were also emphasized. The statement, released by the UN Secretary-General’s office under António Guterres, warned that civilians must not bear the cost of ongoing hostilities, citing past atrocities in El Fasher that were ignored despite prior warnings. It stressed that serious violations of international humanitarian law must not go unpunished and perpetrators must be held accountable. With the approaching holy month of Ramadan, the five organizations urged all parties to seize the opportunity to negotiate a humanitarian truce to reduce violence and enable vital aid delivery. They stated such a truce could serve as a crucial step toward a broader cessation of hostilities. The groups emphasized the need for coordinated, collective, and effective efforts—especially from those with greater influence—to halt the flow of weapons, fighters, and support fueling the violence and fragmenting the country. Looking ahead, they reaffirmed their commitment to facilitating an inclusive, Sudan-led political dialogue to end the war and lay the foundation for a peaceful transition. Since April 15, 2023, Sudan has been engulfed in a devastating war between the army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), triggering the world’s largest humanitarian crisis, with approximately 34 million people in need of assistance—17.3 million of them children. At the peak of fighting, around 14 million people were displaced, both internally and across borders. The conflict has caused tens of thousands of deaths, with some estimates exceeding 150,000. According to the UN’s Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC), over 21 million people face acute food insecurity, and two-thirds of the population urgently require humanitarian aid.

**UAE, African Union Commission Renew Commitment To Enhance Partnership | Independent Newspaper Nigeria**
A high-level meeting was held in Addis Ababa between Mahmoud Ali Youssouf, Chairperson of the African Union Commission (AUC), and Sheikh Shakhboot bin Nahyan Al Nahyan, UAE Minister of State, building on the first round of political consultations in Abu Dhabi on September 13, 2025, under the 2019 Memorandum of Understanding. Both parties reaffirmed their commitment to strengthening the UAE-African Union partnership, emphasizing cooperation on Agenda 2063—particularly the 'Silencing the Guns by 2030' initiative—and the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA), recognizing the interdependence of peace, security, trade, and development. They welcomed the UAE’s $1 billion 'AI for Development' initiative announced at the G20 Leaders’ Summit in Johannesburg in November 2025, noting its potential to advance Africa’s development through digital transformation. The meeting addressed regional security, including the Horn of Africa’s stability and its link to Gulf security, maritime concerns, and the ongoing conflict in Sudan. Both sides called for an immediate, unconditional humanitarian truce, a permanent ceasefire, and unhindered humanitarian access in Sudan, condemned atrocities against civilians, and stressed accountability for violations of international humanitarian law and the need for an independent, civilian-led government. They endorsed the joint AU-IGAD statement of September 14, 2025, and the QUAD Joint Statement of September 12, 2025, and welcomed regional and international humanitarian efforts. On Somalia, they reaffirmed support for its sovereignty and stability. They also condemned Iran’s occupation of the UAE’s islands—Greater Tunb, Lesser Tunb, and Abu Musa—as a violation of UAE sovereignty and the UN Charter, and reiterated support for a peaceful resolution through bilateral negotiations or the International Court of Justice. In preparation for the 2026 AU theme on water and sanitation, they highlighted the 2026 UN Water Conference co-hosted by the UAE and Senegal, agreeing to collaborate on initiatives like the Mohamed bin Zayed Water Initiative to advance water resilience and sustainable development across Africa.

**Sudan pushes fresh plan to end war**
Sudanese Prime Minister Kamil Idris presented a new peace initiative to the UN Security Council on December 24, 2025, aimed at ending the civil war between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), which has been ongoing since April 2023. The plan calls for a phased process beginning with an internationally monitored ceasefire, followed by the withdrawal of RSF fighters from urban areas to designated camps under the supervision of the UN, African Union, and Arab League. The proposal includes disarmament of the RSF, transitional justice measures such as war crimes prosecutions and review of conflict-related legal cases, and the potential integration of some RSF members into national security forces. Humanitarian access would be restored, and displaced civilians would be allowed to return home. The plan builds on a previous roadmap submitted to the UN in March 2025, which included similar provisions: a ceasefire, RSF withdrawal from cities, a three-month humanitarian access window, and civilian return. The conflict originated from tensions between SAF commander Abdel Fattah al-Burhan and RSF commander Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo ‘Hemedti’ over a planned transition to civilian rule. The war has killed tens of thousands and displaced millions. Sudan has accused Kenya of backing the RSF, severed ties with the IGAD regional bloc, and rejected the formation of a rival government by the TASIS coalition in July 2025, which named Dagalo as chairman of a 15-member presidential council—a move rejected by the UN and AU. According to the article, the UN and AU are 'ready to facilitate' peace efforts, with 'such capabilities' to support implementation.

**Jartum Presents UN Peace Proposal for Sudanese Ceasefire and End to War**
Sudan's Prime Minister, Kamel Idris, presented a comprehensive peace proposal to the United Nations Security Council on December 23, 2025, aiming to end the ongoing war in Sudan. The plan centers on a comprehensive ceasefire under joint supervision by the UN, the African Union (AU), and the Arab League, followed by the withdrawal of the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (FAR) from western and central-southern Sudan. The FAR combatants would be regrouped in agreed-upon camps, where their biometric data would be collected to verify nationalities, as Jartum asserts many FAR fighters are foreign mercenaries, including from Colombia and neighboring African countries. The proposal includes facilitating the voluntary return of displaced persons and refugees, ensuring uninterrupted humanitarian aid across conflict-affected areas, and implementing an international-supervised disarmament process to prevent weapons recycling. It also outlines confidence-building measures across political, economic, security, and social dimensions, including immunity from prosecution for civilians or military personnel who sympathized with the FAR but were not involved in war crimes, genocide, or human rights violations, while perpetrators of such crimes would face transitional justice. The plan calls for integrating qualified FAR fighters into government forces and supporting others in reintegrating into civilian life through national reconciliation. Idris pledged a Sudanese-led transitional dialogue among political forces, followed by economic and social development in war-torn regions like Darfur and Kordofán, and free and fair elections under international supervision to complete a democratic transition. The conflict, nearing 1,000 days, has caused tens of thousands of deaths, displaced over 13 million people, and triggered one of the world’s worst humanitarian crises, with over 17 million receiving humanitarian aid in 2025. The presentation coincided with a UN official, Khaled Khiari, describing the war as a 'massacre' and warning of its regional escalation potential.

--- Deep Research Analysis ---
The ceasefire proposals for Sudan in 2026 involve coordinated efforts by the **African Union (AU)**, **United Nations (UN)**, and an **International Quartet** comprising the **United States, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, and the UAE**. 

The primary proposal, presented by **US Advisor Massad Boulos** to the UN Security Council in February 2026, calls for: (1) an immediate humanitarian truce, (2) UN-monitored humanitarian access, (3) phased negotiations for a permanent ceasefire, (4) a structured political process toward civilian-led governance, and (5) reconstruction with accountability for atrocities [23][24]. This builds upon a September 2025 Quartet proposal for a three-month truce followed by a nine-month transitional period [24][26][27].

The **AU Peace and Security Council** issued a statement on February 12, 2026, and AU Commission Chair Mahmoud Ali Youssouf has engaged Saudi officials to support Sudan's sovereignty and reject parallel authorities [21][25][27]. The **UN Security Council** condemned RSF attacks and warned against using starvation as a weapon of war on February 25, 2026 [20][21].

However, the **Sudanese government** has rejected these proposals, insisting any agreement must respect Sudan's "supreme national interests," territorial integrity, and sovereignty [22][23][24][26]. Meanwhile, fighting between the Sudanese Armed Forces and Rapid Support Forces continues with intensified clashes in Kordofan and Darfur [11][13][16].

Notably, **IGAD's specific involvement** was not prominent in the available search results, suggesting the AU has taken the primary continental diplomatic role in these 2026 efforts.

## Summary: El-Fasher's Turning Point – Sudan Between Fragmentation and Escalation

**Source:** Al Jazeera Centre for Studies, 24 November 2025

### Key Developments

- In **late October 2025**, the RSF captured **El-Fasher**, capital of North Darfur, after an 18-month siege. The SAF withdrew a day before the city fell.
- In **March 2025**, the SAF had previously retaken **Khartoum**, restoring control over the political capital — but this required concentrating forces in eastern/central Sudan, leaving Darfur thinly defended.
- The RSF exploited this strategic gap, leveraging Hemedti's Darfuri tribal roots, cross-border supply lines (Libya, Chad, CAR), gold mine revenues (Jebel Amer), and an expanded drone fleet.

### Political Implications

- The RSF's **"Government of Peace and Unity"** (declared April 2025) aims to administer four Darfur states and West Kordofan, issuing currency, passports, and civil registries. Though lacking international recognition, territorial control lends it growing de facto credibility.
- El-Fasher's fall complicates Khartoum's claim to represent all of Sudan and may force external actors to engage Hemedti as a legitimate ruler.

### Humanitarian Situation

- Human Rights Watch and Yale's Humanitarian Research Lab documented **ethnic cleansing, mass executions, and forced displacement** targeting Fur, Zaghawa, and Masalit communities. The U.S. has acknowledged the scale of these atrocities.

### Strategic Outlook

- The SAF faces pressure to retake El-Fasher to prevent RSF military gains from converting into political legitimacy.
- The RSF is expected to launch a **major offensive toward Kordofan** (specifically El-Obeid), a region comprising ~20% of Sudan's landmass, containing the Heglig oilfield, key food production areas, and a potential corridor to Port Sudan.
- However, an RSF push into Kordofan would require redeploying forces from Darfur, leaving El-Fasher vulnerable to SAF counterattack.

### Three Scenarios Noted (without full elaboration in this summary)
The article references three main scenarios for how the conflict may evolve, centered on whether the **Kordofan front stabilizes or escalates** — described as the strategic key to controlling Sudan.

**Overall tone:** No ceasefire or peace process is discussed as imminent or likely; the article frames the conflict as heading toward further escalation and potential fragmentation.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.merip.org/2026/02/burri-under-siege-how-war-remade-everyday-life-in-a-sudanese-neighborhood/">
## Summary of Article: The Siege of Burri and Urban Militarization in Khartoum

**Note:** The article appears to be cut off before completion, so this summary covers only the available content.

### Key Developments
- **March 2025**: The Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) retook key areas of eastern, central, and southern Khartoum, ending the Rapid Support Forces' (RSF) **two-year siege of Burri**, a strategically critical neighborhood in central Khartoum along the Blue Nile River.

### Strategic Significance of Burri
- Burri sits adjacent to major military/political infrastructure: the SAF General Command headquarters, Khartoum International Airport, the presidential palace complex, and key government buildings.
- It connects to other parts of the capital via two critical bridges (Armed Forces Bridge and Al-Manshiya Bridge).

### Conditions During the RSF Siege (Based on Resident Testimonies)
- RSF forces entered Burri within the **first 10 days** of the April 2023 war, using it as a staging ground to attack the General Command.
- Residents faced looting, home seizures, restricted movement via extensive RSF checkpoints, and destruction of food sources, electricity, and water infrastructure.
- RSF forces effectively **redrawn internal boundaries**, sealing off key streets and occupying government buildings along Nile Street.

### Historical Context
- Khartoum's urban layout, dating to **Anglo-Egyptian colonial rule (1899)**, was deliberately designed to facilitate military surveillance and control — a legacy that has shaped the current conflict's geography.
- Successive post-independence regimes, including Omar al-Bashir's (post-1989), further entrenched military presence in the city center.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2025/apr/15/sudans-two-years-of-war-and-its-devastating-toll">
## Summary: Two Years of Sudan's Civil War (Guardian, April 15, 2025)

### Key Facts & Statistics
- **Casualties & Displacement**: The war has killed tens of thousands, displaced more than **13 million people**, and created what the IRC calls "the biggest humanitarian crisis ever recorded"
- **Food insecurity**: More than **20 million people** suffering from acute food insecurity at one point; 750,000+ at risk of famine per outgoing UN aid chief
- **Refugees**: Over **1 million Sudanese** have fled to Chad alone; total displaced (internal + external) reached nearly 13 million by the second anniversary

### Major Military Developments
- RSF initially seized Khartoum's presidential palace and airport (April 2023)
- RSF captured Wad Madani (Sudan's second city) in late 2023
- SAF recaptured Wad Madani and later the presidential palace, gaining control of Khartoum by early 2025
- RSF consolidated control over Darfur territory

### Atrocities & Humanitarian Concerns
- Mass graves discovered near Geneina; ethnic cleansing of Masalit people documented
- **US formally declared RSF committed genocide** against Masalit and non-Arab groups in Darfur
- RSF attacks on displacement camps near El Fasher killed 200+ civilians
- Famine declared in El Fasher displacement camp (housing ~600,000 people)
- National Museum of Khartoum extensively looted

### Peace Efforts
- Only one brief ceasefire (one day, May 2023) occurred, which subsequently collapsed
- The **UK hosted a peace conference** on the second anniversary (April 15, 2025), aiming to find a path to peace — with no reported breakthrough

*Note: The article provides no information about any ceasefire agreements or negotiations in 2026, as it covers events through April 2025.*
</Summary>


<Summary source="https://moderndiplomacy.eu/2026/02/10/ethiopia-secretly-training-sudans-rsf-fighters-sources-say/">
## Summary: Ethiopia Secretly Training RSF Fighters (Modern Diplomacy, February 10, 2026)

### Key Facts & Statistics

- **Ethiopia is secretly training thousands of RSF fighters** at a camp in the **Benishangul-Gumuz region**, approximately 20 miles from the Sudanese border, near the town of Menge
- **~4,300 RSF fighters** are currently receiving training at the camp, per official sources
- The camp has a reported **capacity for up to 10,000 fighters**, according to a November diplomatic cable (source unnamed)
- Satellite imagery confirms construction activity beginning in **April 2025** (forest clearing, metal-roofed structures), with tents appearing in **early November 2025**
- A column of **56 trucks carrying fighters** was observed on November 17, followed by additional convoys
- Recruits are primarily **Ethiopians**, with some from South Sudan and Sudan, including members of the SPLM-N rebel group; some have already crossed into Sudan to fight with the RSF

### External Support & Financing
- The **UAE reportedly financed** the camp's construction and supplied military trainers, though the UAE denied involvement
- **Gorica Group**, an Emirati logistics company, had trucks observed heading toward the camp in October
- **General Getachew Gudina**, Chief of Defense Intelligence of the Ethiopian National Defense Force, is said to have overseen the camp's establishment
- UAE has provided **$3 billion in aid/investments** to Ethiopia since Prime Minister Abiy's rise to power
- A **UAE-Ethiopia MOU** on air and defense capabilities was signed in 2025

### Complicating Factors
- Despite this activity, the **UAE and Ethiopia jointly called for a ceasefire** in Sudan — creating a contradiction between stated diplomatic positions and reported covert military support
- **Asosa airport** (33 miles from camp) has seen new construction since August 2025, including a UAV ground control station, reportedly linked to increased RSF presence in the region
- The camp is approximately **63 miles from the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam (GERD)**, raising concerns about conflict proximity to critical infrastructure
- U.N. experts and U.S. lawmakers have found credible Sudanese army accusations that the **UAE is supplying weapons to the RSF**

### Relevance to Ceasefire Prospects
The article highlights **deepening external military involvement** in Sudan's civil war, with Ethiopia and the UAE covertly bolstering RSF capabilities. This external entrenchment — occurring even as both countries nominally call for a ceasefire — suggests significant obstacles to any genuine cessation of hostilities.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://thearabweekly.com/why-ethiopia-betting-sudans-rsf">
## Article Summary: Ethiopia's Emergence as RSF Patron and Regional Dynamics in Sudan's Civil War

**Note:** The article appears to be cut off at the end, so the summary reflects only the available content.

### Key Facts & Developments

**Ethiopia-RSF Alliance:**
- The SAF intercepted a **150-vehicle convoy** of RSF and allied fighters crossing from Ethiopia into Sudan
- RSF units are reportedly **massing and training inside Ethiopian territory**, specifically in the **Benishangul-Gumuz region** (site of the GERD)
- Ethiopian PM Abiy Ahmed previously received RSF chief Hemedti in Addis Ababa with head-of-state honors
- Sudan's military ally Mustafa Tambour publicly declared Ethiopia "one of the primary supporters of the RSF militia"

**Ethiopia's Motivations:**
- Long-standing accusations that the SAF harbored **TPLF remnants** from the Tigray War (2020–2022)
- Sudan exploited the Tigray crisis in 2020 to reclaim **90% of the disputed al-Fashaqa borderlands**
- Fear that an SAF victory—aligned with Egypt and Eritrea—would threaten **Ethiopian water security** via the GERD

**Egypt's Escalating Involvement:**
- Egypt invoked a **joint defence agreement** with Sudan following the fall of el-Fasher in late 2025
- Egyptian air force reportedly **striking weapons convoys** from Libya heading to resupply the RSF
- Diplomatic pressure contributed to the **temporary closure of the Kufra airbase** in Libya (a key UAE resupply hub)
- Egyptian FM warned Ethiopia: "Any new dams built without prior notification will face an Egyptian reaction"

**Eritrea's Role:**
- SAF reportedly **relocated fighter jets to Eritrean airbases** to protect them from RSF drone strikes
- Eritrea is **hosting and training** SAF-aligned Darfuri and Eastern Sudanese armed groups
- Eritrea deployed **naval assets** to protect Sudan's coast
- Eritrea's support is partly a hedge against Ethiopia's threats to seize the **port of Assab**

**Saudi Arabia vs. UAE Rivalry:**
- The two Gulf powers are now pursuing **opposing regional strategies**
- UAE backs non-state actors (RSF in Sudan, STC in Yemen) to control ports and gold resources
- Saudi Arabia is pursuing a **state-centric approach**, reportedly brokering a **$1.5 billion Pakistan-SAF defence deal** (including light attack aircraft and drones)
- Saudi-led forces struck **Emirati vessels** in Yemen's port of Mukalla in late December, signaling open rivalry

### Key Named Sources
- **Mustafa Tambour** (SAF military ally): Declared Ethiopia an active RSF supporter
- **Egyptian FM Badr Abdelatty**: Issued direct warning to Ethiopia over Nile dam construction
- **Egyptian presidency**: Issued "red lines" statement on preserving Sudan's state institutions (December)

### Relevance to Ceasefire Prospects
The article paints a picture of **deepening regional entrenchment** on both sides of the conflict, with multiple state actors (Ethiopia, UAE, Egypt, Eritrea, Saudi Arabia) now directly or indirectly involved. This **internationalization and proxy-war dynamic** significantly complicates any bilateral ceasefire between the RSF and SAF, as external patrons have competing interests in the war's outcome.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.dw.com/en/sudan-civil-war-ethiopias-uaes-role-under-scrutiny/a-75913225">
## Summary: Sudan Civil War – Ethiopia's and UAE's Role Under Scrutiny (DW)

### Key Facts & Statistics
- A **Reuters report** revealed Ethiopia is hosting a **secret training camp** for RSF fighters, financed by the **UAE**, which also allegedly provided military trainers and logistical support
- The UAE denied involvement, stating it "had no involvement and was not a party to the conflict"
- The UN reports the war has killed **over 40,000 people** and displaced an estimated **14 million**
- The WHO calls the Sudan conflict **"the world's worst health and humanitarian crisis"**
- Over **20 million people** need health assistance; **21 million** face desperate food needs; **33.7 million** will need humanitarian aid in the current year
- **201 attacks on healthcare** have occurred since April 2023, causing 1,858 deaths and 490 injuries
- Famine levels of acute malnutrition reported in **two more towns in Darfur** (IPC, early February)
- Ethiopia hosted **over 50,000 Sudanese refugees** in 2024

### Named Expert Opinions
- **Martin Plaut** (journalist/Horn of Africa expert): Described the UAE as "the main driver" of the conflict; said "it would take a miracle to end the ongoing conflict"; noted neither side will run out of equipment, money, or personnel given their respective backers (RSF: UAE, Libya, Chad, Ethiopia; SAF: Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Somalia) — concluding **"I don't see any end to the conflict"**
- **Abdurahman Seid** (geopolitical analyst, Horn of Africa): Confirmed RSF set up camp in western Ethiopia approximately two months prior to the article

### Implications for Ceasefire Prospects
The article strongly suggests **deep entrenchment of foreign backing on both sides**, with expert opinion explicitly pessimistic about any near-term resolution or ceasefire.
</Summary>
